Ariba Buying & Invoicing at 43% utilization is materially below peer healthcare customers — a P2P adoption sprint is warranted.
qbr.recommended_ask: Commitment to a joint CX adoption diagnostic in Q3 covering Cloud for Customer and Incentive Management — root cause and 90-day adoption plan with executive sponsor.

questions_they_will_ask.question: Should we expand Integration Suite or wait?
questions_they_will_ask.suggested_answer: You are at 103% of contract — you are already over. An expansion conversation is appropriate now, and we should pair it with a HANA Cloud and Data Intelligence Cloud workload review to size the full data platform footprint for FY27.

questions_they_will_ask.question: Why is our Ariba P2P utilization lower than industry peers?
questions_they_will_ask.suggested_answer: Comparable healthcare customers in our portfolio run Ariba Buying at 89-100% contract utilization; Abbott is at 43%. A short transaction-level audit can quantify how much spend is still flowing through non-PO invoices and manual approval chains.

ea_recommended_actions: Frame Integration Suite expansion conversation for FY27 based on 103% utilization
Diagnose Cloud for Customer and Incentive Management adoption gaps before renewal cycle
Quantify maverick spend on Ariba Buying & Invoicing through AP transaction audit

Cardinal Health Inc..architectural_summary: Cardinal's operational stack — Concur T&E (all modules 107-131%) and Ariba P2P core (Buying & Invoicing at 100% utilization) — is healthy. The defining risk is SAP Commerce Cloud at 8.0% attainment, 4.4% contract utilization on a $659K contract: this is a near-failed deployment of a direct revenue-generating capability. Ariba Contracts at 58% utilization and Supplier Lifecycle at 8% utilization point to procurement governance gaps that mirror AbbVie's pattern. SAP Build at 68.4% with downward trend suggests the citizen-developer layer is not gaining traction. The pattern is operationally solid but with a major commerce platform exposure that needs immediate executive attention.

key_signals.title: SAP Commerce Cloud at 4.4% contract utilization on $659K contract
key_signals.pattern: Commerce Cloud YTD: $28.7K consumed of $359.8K budget (8% attainment), $28.7K of $658.7K contract (4.4% utilization). Monthly run-rate is roughly $7K against a $90K target.
key_signals.explanation: This is the single highest-dollar architectural risk in the Cardinal portfolio. Commerce Cloud is a direct revenue-generating platform — this level of underconsumption signals either a failed go-live, an indefinitely delayed deployment, or active migration to a competing commerce platform. With 7 months remaining, recovery is mathematically very difficult. The renewal conversation needs to start now.
key_signals.action: Immediate joint discovery with Cardinal commerce leadership to confirm deployment status, competing platform exposure, and a credible path to either rightsize or restart the deployment.

key_signals.title: Procurement governance layer underused while transactional Ariba runs hot
key_signals.pattern: Ariba Buying & Invoicing at 100% contract utilization, but Contracts at 58.4% and Supplier Lifecycle at 8.0%. Invoices are being processed against suppliers and contracts that are not managed in the system.
key_signals.explanation: The transactional procurement layer is fully consumed but the governance layer (CLM and supplier qualification) is not. This is a compliance and audit pattern — Cardinal is running invoices and POs that lack traceability to qualified suppliers and managed contracts.
key_signals.action: Initiate a 100-invoice sample audit to quantify the percentage referencing suppliers without active Lifecycle records or contracts not in Ariba Contracts.

qbr.opening: Cardinal's operational SAP estate is healthy — Concur and Ariba P2P are at or above plan — and this QBR needs to surface the Commerce Cloud deployment status as a strategic conversation.
key_points: Concur T&E module suite running 107-131% attainment — operationally exemplary.
Ariba Buying & Invoicing at 100% contract utilization; Contracts (58%) and Supplier Lifecycle (8%) are the governance gaps.
SAP Commerce Cloud at 4.4% contract utilization on a $659K commitment requires immediate joint diagnostic.
qbr.recommended_ask: Executive sponsor commitment to a Commerce Cloud deployment status review within 30 days with a written go/no-go decision on the platform path forward.

questions_they_will_ask.question: What can we do about the Commerce Cloud underconsumption?
questions_they_will_ask.suggested_answer: The first question is whether the deployment is live, delayed, or being replaced — the answer determines whether we restart enablement, rightsize the contract, or co-design a transition plan. We need a joint executive review in 30 days to make that decision before renewal pressure builds.

questions_they_will_ask.question: Why is Supplier Lifecycle so low when Ariba transactions are at 100%?
questions_they_will_ask.suggested_answer: It means invoices are flowing against suppliers that may not be qualified or risk-assessed in the system. For a regulated distribution business, that's an audit exposure. A targeted onboarding sprint for your top 100 suppliers would close the gap in a quarter.

ea_recommended_actions: Schedule Commerce Cloud deployment status review with Cardinal commerce leadership within 10 business days
Run invoice-to-supplier reconciliation to quantify governance gap in Supplier Lifecycle
Audit Ariba Sourcing event volume to confirm whether RFx are running offline

Medtronic, Inc..architectural_summary: Medtronic shows the most contradictory architecture in the portfolio: BTP Enterprise Agreement at 518% attainment in Joule Studio sub-area (uncontrolled platform consumption) while zero in BDC Public, Database Cloud, FP&A, Integration Suite, and Tech Foundation sub-areas — signaling fragmented BTP usage without governance. SAP Commerce Cloud at 8.4% / 4.1% utilization on a $790K contract mirrors Cardinal's failure pattern. Ariba P2P core (Buying & Invoicing at 100%, Buying at 81%) and Concur T&E are healthy. Watchlist Screening at zero consumption is an active trade compliance exposure for a global medical device manufacturer. Integration Suite is declining (55.9% attainment, downward trend) while BTP runs hot — the two should correlate.

key_signals.title: BTP EA fragmented — 518% in one sub-area, zero in five others
key_signals.pattern: BTP shows $17K consumed against $3.3K budget in Joule Studio (518%), and zero against $1.5K-$2.2K budgets in BDC Public, Database Cloud, Integration Suite, FP&A, and Tech Foundation Other.
key_signals.explanation: This pattern indicates ungoverned BTP consumption — likely orphaned sandboxes, test environments, or a single team consuming heavily while the rest of the organization has not activated. Meanwhile Integration Suite is declining (55.9%, downward trend) and Build is at 57% — the foundation is being consumed without the application layer benefiting. The EA should investigate whether BTP metering is properly attributed across services.
key_signals.action: Request BTP metering breakdown by service (Integration, Build, HANA, Joule) for last 90 days to isolate the 5x overage driver and reconcile against active projects.

key_signals.title: SAP Commerce Cloud at 4.1% utilization on $790K contract; Preferred Success at 100%
key_signals.pattern: Commerce Cloud at 8.4% attainment / 4.1% utilization on a $790K contract, while Preferred Success Commerce runs at 114.2% attainment / 100% utilization.
key_signals.explanation: The premium support entitlement is being consumed in full while the platform it supports is essentially unused. This signals either Preferred Success hours are being spent on stabilization of a stalled deployment, or on supporting a non-SAP commerce platform. Either way, the $790K Commerce Cloud contract is at material renewal risk.
key_signals.action: Pull Preferred Success Commerce ticket categorization for last 5 months to determine whether support is addressing Commerce Cloud deployment blockers or unrelated work.

key_signals.title: Watchlist Screening at zero consumption — trade compliance exposure
key_signals.pattern: Watchlist Screening shows zero actuals against $1.8K YTD budget on a $3.4K contract. Ariba Supplier Risk has no consumption data.
key_signals.explanation: For a global medical device manufacturer, restricted/denied party screening is a direct trade compliance and sanctions exposure. Zero consumption means screening is either manual (high error risk) or not happening systematically. This is a compliance question, not a cost question.
key_signals.action: Engage Medtronic's Trade Compliance lead to confirm whether screening is running through another system or is in fact manual — and document the activation blockers.

qbr.opening: Medtronic's operational Ariba and Concur estate is performing well, and this QBR should focus the executive conversation on BTP governance, Commerce Cloud direction, and the compliance posture of Watchlist Screening.
key_points: Ariba Buying & Invoicing at 100% contract utilization; Buying at 81% — strong P2P adoption.
BTP EA shows fragmented consumption — 518% in one sub-area, zero in five — pointing to governance issues.
SAP Commerce Cloud at 4.1% utilization on $790K and Watchlist Screening at zero are the two highest-priority strategic conversations.
qbr.recommended_ask: Executive commitment to (1) a BTP governance review naming a platform owner and (2) a 30-day decision review on Commerce Cloud deployment path.

questions_they_will_ask.question: Why is BTP consumption so erratic across sub-areas?
questions_they_will_ask.suggested_answer: It suggests one team is consuming heavily — likely Joule or Integration sandboxes — while the broader platform has no governance, no central owner, and no enabled use cases in other LOBs. A BTP center of excellence with a named platform lead is the standard fix.

questions_they_will_ask.question: Is Commerce Cloud salvageable for this fiscal year?
questions_they_will_ask.suggested_answer: Mathematically, recovering $725K of consumption in 7 months on a platform consuming $10K/month is not realistic. The right conversation is whether to restart with a defined go-live, rightsize the contract, or co-design a transition. We need that decision in 30 days.

ea_recommended_actions: Pull BTP metering breakdown to attribute the 518% Joule Studio overage to specific projects
Validate Watchlist Screening activation blocker and engage Trade Compliance lead
Frame Commerce Cloud decision review with executive sponsor before Q4

renewal_risks.recommended_engagement: Medtronic's Digital Commerce leadership and CIO — 30-day decision review on whether to restart, rightsize, or transition the deployment.

renewal_risks.recommended_engagement: AbbVie's FP&A lead and SAC admin — current-state planning swimlane and 90-day migration plan.

renewal_risks.recommended_engagement: Medtronic's Trade Compliance and GRC lead — activation diagnostic and 90-day go-live.

architectural_signals.title: SAP Commerce Cloud structurally underdeployed at two customers totaling $1.45M
architectural_signals.pattern: Cardinal consumes $28.7K of $659K (4.4%), Medtronic consumes $32.5K of $790K (4.1%). Both are running monthly at roughly 8-10% of target. At Medtronic, Preferred Success Commerce runs at 100% utilization while the platform itself is dormant.
architectural_signals.explanation: This is the single largest renewal exposure in the portfolio. Commerce Cloud is a direct revenue-generating platform — when consumption is this low, the deployment is either failed, indefinitely delayed, or being replaced by a competing platform. The Medtronic Preferred Success pattern is particularly diagnostic: paid support resources are being spent without the platform being live, which usually means stabilization of a stalled implementation or work on an unrelated platform. Both customers need an executive-level decision review before renewal cycles begin.
architectural_signals.action_for_ea: Within 10 business days, schedule joint architecture and commerce operations reviews with both Cardinal and Medtronic to confirm deployment status, competing platform exposure, and produce a written path forward (restart, rightsize, or transition).

architectural_signals.title: BTP Enterprise Agreement unactivated or ungoverned at two customers
architectural_signals.pattern: AbbVie shows zero BTP consumption across BDC Public, Database Cloud, Joule Studio, Integration Suite, and Tech Foundation sub-areas. Medtronic shows 518% consumption in Joule Studio but zero across the other five sub-areas. Both customers also show underperforming Build, SAC, and Integration Suite downstream.
architectural_signals.explanation: BTP is the runtime foundation for extensibility, integration, and Joule AI. At AbbVie the foundation is contractually in place but operationally unactivated — every downstream cloud capability is therefore underrealized. At Medtronic the consumption pattern is the opposite problem: a single team is consuming heavily without governance while the broader platform has no owner. Both patterns block the AI roadmap and the clean-core extensibility story, just for different reasons. The EA work is to diagnose which pattern applies and address the root cause.
architectural_signals.action_for_ea: For AbbVie: request BTP tenant provisioning and identity federation status within 14 days. For Medtronic: pull BTP metering breakdown by service (Integration Suite, Build, HANA Cloud, Joule) for last 90 days to isolate the 5x overage driver.

architectural_signals.title: Ariba procurement governance layer underused across three customers
architectural_signals.pattern: AbbVie Supplier Lifecycle at 15.6% contract utilization while Ariba Buying & Invoicing runs at 99%. Cardinal Supplier Lifecycle at 8% and Ariba Contracts at 58% while Buying & Invoicing runs at 100%. Abbott Supplier Risk at 11.6%. The transactional procurement layer is fully consumed at all three; the governance layer is not.
architectural_signals.explanation: This is a portfolio-wide pattern: invoices and POs are flowing against suppliers that are not fully qualified, contracts that are not managed in the system, and risk profiles that are not being monitored. For three regulated healthcare/life sciences customers with global supply chains, this is both an audit exposure and a compliance gap. It is also a repeatable enablement motion — the same workshop sequence (supplier master reconciliation, top-N onboarding sprint, risk policy configuration) applies to all three.
architectural_signals.action_for_ea: Design a single repeatable procurement governance enablement playbook covering Supplier Lifecycle onboarding, Contracts migration, and Supplier Risk policy configuration; deliver to AbbVie, Cardinal, and Abbott in Q3 FY2026.

architectural_signals.title: Abbott Integration Suite at contract ceiling signals data platform expansion
architectural_signals.pattern: Integration Suite at 106.8% attainment / 103% contract utilization, Data Intelligence Cloud at 99.1% / 71.6%, HANA Cloud at 65% / 49.7%. Integration is at ceiling; data layer has headroom.
architectural_signals.explanation: Integration Suite over-consumption is a clear true-up trigger. The fact that DIC and HANA Cloud are not yet saturated means there is data engineering and analytics workload headroom that could be migrated to the SAP data platform alongside the integration expansion. This is the strongest pure expansion opportunity in the portfolio.
architectural_signals.action_for_ea: Frame an architecture review with Abbott's integration and data platform leads to size FY27 Integration Suite expansion and map on-premise data engineering workloads ready for migration to HANA Cloud / Data Intelligence Cloud.

momentum.title: IBP at exactly 100% utilization — model supply chain deployment
momentum.why_architecturally: IBP at 100% of contract with flat trend indicates steady-state operational use across demand sensing, inventory optimization, and S&OP. This is the anchor product in Abbott's supply chain architecture and a reference deployment for the rest of the portfolio.
momentum.expand_opportunity: Use IBP maturity as the anchor for a Joule / supply chain AI expansion conversation in FY27 — specifically demand sensing AI enhancements and predictive inventory optimization.

momentum.title: Integration Suite at contract ceiling — expansion trigger
momentum.why_architecturally: 103% contract utilization on a foundational integration platform means Abbott is actively expanding its integration footprint and is at capacity. This is the clearest expansion signal in the portfolio.
momentum.expand_opportunity: Size an Integration Suite expansion for FY27 paired with a HANA Cloud / DIC workload migration review.

momentum.title: Concur module suite over-performing across three customers
momentum.why_architecturally: Cardinal's Concur stack runs at 107-131% attainment across all modules with 95% contract utilization — this is an exemplary T&E deployment and a reference pattern that can be cited at AbbVie and Medtronic Concur reviews.
momentum.expand_opportunity: Leverage Cardinal's Concur reference to expand the Preferred Success engagement model into underperforming Ariba and SAC adoption sprints at the same customer.

momentum.title: SAC BI overconsumption at Abbott — adoption ahead of contract
momentum.why_architecturally: 107.5% attainment and 107.5% contract utilization with a downward trend signals users are consuming beyond contract — a healthy adoption signal but with capacity tension. Right-sizing the contract would unlock continued growth.
momentum.expand_opportunity: Initiate a contract true-up conversation and frame an SAC Planning expansion alongside, capitalizing on the BI adoption momentum.

ai_narrative: Portfolio at 78.1% YTD attainment across $7.66M target, but the headline masks two critical signals: SAP Commerce Cloud is dormant at Cardinal (4.4% utilization on $659K) and Medtronic (4.1% on $790K), and BTP EA is unactivated across AbbVie and Medtronic — blocking the entire extensibility and AI roadmap. Concur and Ariba P2P backbones are healthy across all four customers; the risk concentration is in strategic platform contracts, not operational SaaS.
